project.name: iora_codecs
project.description: General-purpose audio and video codec library for media encoding, decoding, and DSP, built on the Iora framework
project.buildSystem: CMake (all external dependencies as git submodules under libs/)
project.license: TBD (all dependencies are permissive except bcg729 (GPL-3.0), which is confined to the optional mod_g729.so module via RTLD_LOCAL isolation)

designPrinciples: Reuse Iora framework objects wherever possible — threading, timers, queues, logging, config, network, lifecycle
Extend Iora with new primitives only when no existing object can serve the purpose
Plugin-based codec registration matching Iora's plugin architecture
Zero-copy media pipeline where feasible — minimize allocations in the hot path
Separation of codec, DSP, and pipeline layers
Transport-agnostic — this library handles codecs and media processing only; signaling and transport (SIP, WebRTC, SRTP, ICE) belong in separate libraries that consume this one
Expose clean interfaces so that higher-level transport libraries (e.g., a future libdatachannel bridge or SIP stack) can plug in seamlessly
Device I/O abstraction provided by the companion iora_io_device library for seamless integration with audio/video capture and playback hardware
Each codec is a loadable module — applications link only the codecs they need

layers.overview: The library is organized into three distinct layers, each depending only on layers below it. Transport, signaling, RTP framing, and device I/O are explicitly out of scope — they belong in companion libraries that share iora_codecs_core types.

stack.name: Media Pipeline
stack.description: Orchestration layer — connects codecs, DSP stages, and external sources/sinks into processing graphs

stack.name: DSP
stack.description: Resampling, gain, VAD, DTMF tone generation/detection, WAV file I/O — audio signal processing utilities

stack.name: Iora Core (external)
stack.description: Threading, timers, logging, config, network transport, lifecycle, crypto — provided by Iora

outOfScope: SIP signaling and SDP offer/answer negotiation
RTP packet parsing/building, jitter buffering, DTMF, comfort noise — companion iora_rtp library
RTP transport (socket send/receive) — belongs in iora_rtp or a transport library
Audio/video device I/O (capture, playback, enumeration) — companion iora_io_device library
libdatachannel integration — will be a separate bridge library
SDP generation/parsing — will be handled by the signaling library

ioraReuse.description: Iora objects reused directly by iora_codecs — no wrapping or reimplementation

iora::core::ThreadPool.notes: Work-stealing pool with dynamic sizing is ideal for variable codec workloads

iora::core::TimerService.notes: Microsecond precision via epoll/timerfd is sufficient for audio frame timing (20ms granularity)

usedBy: Non-real-time video frame queues (e.g., decode output to display)
iora::core::BlockingQueue<T>.notes: Bounded queue with timeout provides natural backpressure. Uses std::mutex internally — NOT suitable for the real-time audio hot path. For audio pipeline stages, use RingBuffer<T> instead. BlockingQueue is appropriate for control plane operations and non-latency-critical paths where backpressure semantics (blocking on full) are more important than lock-free performance.

usedBy: Diagnostic and monitoring events (codec stats export, call quality reports)
iora::core::EventQueue.notes: JSON event model fits diagnostic and inter-process events. NOT suitable for high-frequency real-time pipeline events (DTMF digits, VAD state changes, key frame requests) due to JSON serialization overhead. For real-time pipeline events, iora_codecs defines typed event structs with std::variant — see MediaEvent in pipeline abstractions.

usedBy: All layers — diagnostics, packet loss logging, codec stats, device errors
iora::core::Logger.notes: Async mode preferred for media path to avoid blocking

iora::core::ConfigLoader.notes: TOML config format for codec module settings

iora::network::ObjectPool<T>.notes: Generic pooling reduces allocation churn in the media hot path

usedBy: Codec fallback on repeated encode/decode failures
iora::network::CircuitBreaker.notes: Fault tolerance pattern for unreliable codec paths

iora::common::ILifecycleManaged.notes: Standard Created→Running→Draining→Stopped→Reset state machine for pipeline objects. ICodec does NOT implement ILifecycleManaged — codecs are lightweight construction-ready/destruction-cleanup objects. Device lifecycle is managed by iora_io_device.

usedBy: SSRC generation in iora_rtp for RTP packet assembly
iora::crypto::SecureRng.notes: OpenSSL-based CSPRNG. Used by iora_rtp (not iora_codecs) for SSRC generation. SSRC uniqueness (not cryptographic strength) is the requirement per RFC 3550 — std::random_device seeding std::mt19937 is an acceptable fallback if SecureRng requires OpenSSL at link time.

usedBy: Optional RTCP stats aggregation with TTL-based cleanup (in iora_rtp)
iora::util::ExpiringCache<K,V>.notes: TTL-based expiry is useful for caching results from external systems. NOT used for CodecRegistry lookups — registered codecs do not expire, so a simple std::unordered_map is more appropriate and avoids unnecessary TTL overhead on the codec hot path.

iora::ids::Uuid.notes: UUID v7 (time-ordered) for session correlation in logs

iora::parsers::Json.notes: Zero-dependency JSON for structured data exchange

iora::storage::JsonFileStore.notes: Optional — only for long-running diagnostic collection

usedBy: Device driver plugins in iora_io_device (ALSA, PulseAudio, V4L2)
iora::common::PluginLoader.notes: Iora's PluginLoader wraps dlopen/dlsym with RTLD_NOW | RTLD_LOCAL. Shared state (Logger, IoraService, JsonFileStore) is unified through libiora-core.so linkage — each plugin links against the same .so at build time. RTLD_LOCAL provides symbol isolation between plugins, which enables loading GPL-licensed codecs (e.g., bcg729/G.729) without polluting the global symbol namespace or propagating GPL obligations to the host. Codec modules are loaded as Iora plugins — each .so exports a factory that registers with CodecRegistry during onLoad().

iora::common::PluginManager.notes: Manages loaded plugins and their lifecycle. CodecRegistry delegates plugin discovery to PluginManager.

ioraExtensions.description: New primitives that must be added to the Iora base framework — only those general-purpose enough to belong in Iora itself, not in iora_codecs. Currently none required by iora_codecs.
ioraExtensions._note_ringbuffer: iora::core::RingBuffer<T> (lock-free SPSC circular buffer) was previously listed here but its primary consumer is JitterBuffer in iora_rtp. It will be proposed as an Iora extension when iora_rtp is implemented.

externalDependencies.description: All external libraries managed as git submodules under libs/. Iora itself is also a submodule. CMake add_subdirectory() or ExternalProject_Add is used to build each submodule. This matches the pattern used in the karoo_sbc project.

integrationMethod.cmakeIntegration: add_subdirectory() for CMake-native libs, ExternalProject_Add for autotools/Meson libs
integrationMethod.pinning: Each submodule is pinned to a specific tag/commit for reproducible builds

iora.cmakeIntegration: Manual INTERFACE library pointing at libs/iora/include (iora's own CMakeLists.txt is not designed for add_subdirectory — it uses CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR and always builds the iora executable)
iora.purpose: Base framework — threading, timers, logging, config, network, lifecycle, crypto

libopus.purpose: Opus encode/decode — mandatory modern VoIP audio codec
libopus.notes: Use OPUS_APPLICATION_VOIP for telephony. Can run at 8kHz for efficient bridging with G.711.

bcg729.cmakeIntegration: add_subdirectory(libs/bcg729) with ENABLE_UNIT_TESTS=OFF, ENABLE_STRICT=OFF, BUILD_SHARED_LIBS=OFF — builds as static library linked PRIVATE into mod_g729.so
bcg729.purpose: G.729 Annex A encode/decode — narrowband 8 kbps codec, patents expired 2017
bcg729.gplIsolation: GPL-3.0 scope is confined to mod_g729.so only. bcg729 is statically linked into the plugin with PRIVATE CMake linkage. RTLD_LOCAL plugin loading (enabled by libiora-core.so) prevents GPL symbols from leaking into the host or other modules. IORA_CODECS_ENABLE_G729=OFF by default — users opt in explicitly.
bcg729.notes: G.729 was previously excluded because Iora required RTLD_GLOBAL for shared singletons. The singleton-removal project (libiora-core.so) enabled RTLD_LOCAL, making GPL isolation feasible. G.729 patents expired worldwide by January 2017.

g711.notes: Trivial — ~40 lines of code. No external library needed. Implement inline.

libg722.cmakeIntegration: Custom CMakeLists.txt wrapping the .c/.h files, or compile sources directly into the g722 codec module
libg722.purpose: G.722 wideband encode/decode — 7 kHz audio at 64 kbps
features: 16 kHz audio (8 kHz RTP clock — historical error)
libg722.notes: Small enough to compile directly. A few .c/.h files.

libilbc.buildSystem: CMake (requires abseil-cpp submodule: git submodule update --init --recursive)
libilbc.purpose: iLBC encode/decode — codec with good packet loss resilience
libilbc.notes: Still encountered in legacy deployments and useful for high packet-loss environments.

opencore-amr.cmakeIntegration: ExternalProject_Add with configure/make, or use GitHub mirror with CMake support
opencore-amr.purpose: AMR-NB encode/decode, AMR-WB decode — 3GPP mobile codec for VoLTE interworking
opencore-amr.notes: Patent encumbered — Via Licensing patent license may be required for commercial deployment. AMR-WB encoder available separately via vo-amrwbenc.

vo-amrwbenc.purpose: AMR-WB (G.722.2) encoder — companion to opencore-amr's decoder

libspeexdsp.cmakeIntegration: Direct source compilation — only resample.c is compiled into a static library (speexdsp) with a generated speexdsp_config_types.h providing platform type definitions. No ExternalProject_Add or autotools needed.
libspeexdsp.purpose: Audio resampler (speex_resampler) — NOT the Speex codec, just the DSP utilities
libspeexdsp.notes: The speex_resampler is the de facto standard audio resampler in open source. Essential for bridging 48kHz Opus with 8kHz G.711. Only speex_resampler.c and speex_resampler.h are needed — can be compiled directly.

openh264.cmakeIntegration: ExternalProject_Add with make, or use Cisco prebuilt binaries
openh264.purpose: H.264 encode/decode — Cisco pays MPEG-LA royalties for prebuilt binaries
openh264.notes: Royalty-free ONLY when using Cisco's prebuilt binaries. If compiling from source, user must obtain separate MPEG-LA license.

libvpx.notes: Not CMake-native. Uses its own configure/make build. ExternalProject_Add is the correct integration pattern. Google's royalty-free patent commitment covers all users.

libaom.cmakeIntegration: ExternalProject_Add (add_subdirectory pollutes global CMAKE_C_FLAGS with -Werror, breaking other submodules)
libaom.purpose: AV1 encode/decode — next-generation royalty-free video codec
libaom.notes: CPU-intensive. Real-time encode viable on modern hardware. Decode is lightweight. dav1d is a faster decoder-only alternative.

dav1d.purpose: AV1 decode only — fastest available AV1 decoder
dav1d.notes: Use dav1d for decode + libaom for encode if AV1 support is needed. Meson build system requires ExternalProject_Add.

coreAbstractions.description: Core interfaces and classes defined by iora_codecs

MediaBuffer.description: Reference-counted, poolable byte buffer with metadata for media frames
requirements: Pre-allocated from a pool (integrates with iora::network::ObjectPool)
Reference-counted via std::shared_ptr with custom deleter returning to pool — enables shared ownership for fan-out scenarios (e.g., recording + playback simultaneously)
Metadata: timestamp (uint32_t), sequence number (uint16_t), SSRC (uint32_t), payload type (uint8_t), marker bit (bool), capture time (steady_clock::time_point)
Capacity vs size distinction (allocated capacity, used size)
Data pointer access without copy (data(), size(), capacity())

Ownership model: shared_ptr-based shared ownership (NOT move-only — shared_ptr and move-only are contradictory). Shared ownership is needed for fan-out; single-owner pipelines naturally degrade to single shared_ptr holder

MediaClock.description: Media clock for timestamp generation and synchronization across pipeline stages
requirements: Monotonic clock source based on std::chrono::steady_clock
Configurable clock rate (e.g., 8000 Hz, 48000 Hz, 90000 Hz) for RTP timestamp generation
Wall-clock to media-timestamp conversion and vice versa
Drift measurement between two MediaClock instances (e.g., capture vs playout)

ICodec.description: Abstract interface for all audio and video codecs
ICodec.lifecycle: Does NOT implement iora::common::ILifecycleManaged — codec instances are lightweight encode/decode engines. Construction = ready to use; destruction = cleanup.

interface.info(): Returns CodecInfo (name, type, clockRate, channels, payloadType, bitrate, frameSize)
interface.encode(inputBuffer): Encode raw samples/pixels into compressed frame, returns MediaBuffer
interface.decode(inputBuffer): Decode compressed frame into raw samples/pixels, returns MediaBuffer
interface.plc(frameSamples): Packet loss concealment — generate synthetic frame

CodecInfo.description: Descriptor for codec identity and capabilities

fields.name: string — e.g., 'opus', 'PCMU', 'H264'
fields.clockRate: uint32_t — RTP clock rate in Hz
fields.defaultPayloadType: uint8_t — static PT or 0 for dynamic
fields.frameSize: std::chrono::microseconds — duration of one codec frame (e.g., 20ms)
fields.features: bitfield — FEC, DTX, VAD, PLC, VBR, CBR, SVC

ICodecFactory.description: Factory for creating codec instances — one factory per codec implementation

interface.codecInfo(): Returns CodecInfo for the codec this factory produces
interface.supports(info): Check if this factory can produce a codec matching the given info

CodecRegistry.description: Central registry for all available codecs — integrates with Iora plugin system

interface.registerFactory(factory): Register a codec factory (called by codec plugins on load)
interface.createEncoder(info): Look up factory by CodecInfo and create encoder
interface.createDecoder(info): Look up factory by CodecInfo and create decoder
interface.findByName(name): Find codec by SDP encoding name
interface.findByPayloadType(pt): Find codec by RTP payload type
interface._note_no_negotiate: SDP codec negotiation is explicitly NOT part of CodecRegistry — it belongs in the signaling library. Signaling libraries use enumerateCodecs/findByName/findByPayloadType to query available codecs and perform their own offer/answer intersection with full SDP context.

CodecRegistry.notes: Owned by the mod_codec_registry Iora plugin module (modules/registry/mod_codec_registry.cpp). The host application loads mod_codec_registry first, which creates the CodecRegistry and exports it as 'codecs.registry'. Codec plugins access it in onLoad() via service->callExportedApi<CodecRegistry&>('codecs.registry') and call registerFactory() to auto-register. If mod_codec_registry is not loaded, codec modules still function — their factories are accessible via per-module exported APIs (e.g., 'g711.pcmu.factory', 'opus.factory') — but they will not appear in the central registry. mod_codec_registry also exports convenience APIs: 'codecs.registry.enumerate', 'codecs.registry.findByName', and 'codecs.registry.findByPayloadType'. The ICodec* returned from createEncoder/createDecoder is used directly via C++ virtual dispatch for the hot path (encode/decode/plc) — per-frame calls never go through the exported API system.

Resampler.description: Audio sample rate converter wrapping libspeexdsp's speex_resampler

interface.process(input, output): Convert sample rate, returns samples written
interface.reset(): Reset internal state (e.g., on SSRC change)

AudioMixer.description: Audio mixing engine — takes N decoded PCM input streams and produces per-participant output streams where each participant hears all OTHER participants (N-1 mix semantics for conference calls)

interface.AudioMixer(params): Constructor with MixParams (target sample rate, channels, max participants, algorithm)
interface.pushAudio(participantId, buffer): Submit a decoded PCM frame from one participant
interface.mixFor(participantId): Returns a mixed MediaBuffer containing audio from all OTHER participants (N-1 mix)

algorithms.SampleAverage: Simple average of all N-1 input samples — prevents clipping by dividing by source count
algorithms.SaturatingAdd: Sum samples with int16_t saturation clamping — louder but clips with many participants
algorithms.AgcNormalized: Automatic gain control after summing — maintains consistent output level regardless of participant count

AudioMixer.inputFormat: S16 PCM (int16_t) — all inputs must be S16 at the target sample rate. TranscodingHandler upstream handles codec decoding and resampling. AudioMixer does NOT perform format conversion.
designNotes: Per-participant Resampler instances normalize all inputs to a common target sample rate before mixing
Pre-allocated intermediate buffers (one per participant + one output) avoid per-frame heap allocations

N-1 mix: for each participant P, mix = sum of all OTHER participants' audio. This requires either N separate mix passes or a single sum with per-participant subtraction (subtract P's contribution from the total sum)
Efficient N-1 via subtraction: compute total_sum once, then for each participant P: output_P = total_sum - P. This is O(N) instead of O(N^2)

Accumulator type: total_sum MUST be int32_t (not int16_t) to prevent overflow when summing N participants. Example: 4 participants at max amplitude (32767) → sum = 131068, which overflows int16_t. Final per-participant output is clamped to int16_t range [-32768, 32767] after subtraction and optional division.
NOT thread-safe — caller must synchronize pushAudio() and mixFor() calls. Typical pattern: all pushAudio() calls complete, then all mixFor() calls, within a single timer tick

Gain.description: Header-only audio gain/volume control with saturation clamping, mute/unmute, and dB conversion

interface.Gain(factor): Constructor — linear gain factor (0.0 = silence, 1.0 = unity). Rejects negative, NaN, and infinity values.
interface.apply(samples, sampleCount): Apply gain to S16 PCM samples in-place with saturation clamping to [-32768, 32767]
interface.apply(MediaBuffer&): Convenience — applies gain to a MediaBuffer's S16 PCM data. Rejects odd-byte buffers (not aligned to int16_t).
interface.setGain(factor): Set linear gain factor (validates input, updates mute-stored value)
interface.gainDb(): Get current gain in decibels (returns -infinity for zero gain)
interface.mute(): Mute — stores current gain and sets effective gain to 0

Gain.handler: GainHandler — IMediaHandler wrapper that applies Gain to both incoming and outgoing MediaBuffers
designNotes: Float-domain comparison before int cast prevents int32_t overflow UB on extreme gain factors
Null pointer guard on apply() for defensive programming
setGain() while muted updates the stored unmute value so the new gain takes effect on unmute

Vad.description: Header-only energy-based Voice Activity Detection with RMS threshold, hangover logic, and speech onset marking

interface.process(samples, sampleCount): Analyze frame — returns VadResult {isActive, rmsEnergy, peakAmplitude}
interface.process(MediaBuffer&): Convenience — processes a MediaBuffer's S16 PCM data

Vad.handler: VadHandler — IMediaHandler with separate Vad instances per direction (incoming/outgoing)

modes.DROP_SILENT: Silent frames are not forwarded to the next handler
modes.MARK_ONLY: All frames forwarded — speech onset frames get the RFC 3550 §5.1 marker bit set

designNotes: Separate _vadIncoming and _vadOutgoing instances prevent cross-direction state interference
Counter overflow protection on _consecutiveSilenceFrames (uint32_t max guard)
Marker bit set only on speech onset (transition from inactive→active), not on continuation

ToneGenerator.description: Header-only DTMF tone synthesizer per ITU-T Q.23 — generates dual-tone signals for all 16 DTMF digits

interface.generate(digit, durationMs, amplitude): Generate a single DTMF tone as a MediaBuffer. Returns nullptr for invalid digits or zero duration.
interface.generateSilence(durationMs): Generate a silence buffer. Returns nullptr for zero duration.
interface.generateSequence(digits, toneDurationMs,: Generate a sequence of tones with inter-digit silence gaps
interface.dtmfFrequencies(digit): Static — returns {lowFreq, highFreq} pair for a DTMF digit

designNotes: Double-precision phase accumulator prevents frequency drift
uint64_t widening for sampleRate*durationMs prevents overflow at 48kHz/90s
Saturation clamping via int32_t intermediate before int16_t cast
Supports all 16 DTMF digits: 0-9, *, #, A, B, C, D

GoertzelDetector.description: Header-only DTMF detector using the Goertzel algorithm — O(N) per-frequency DFT magnitude computation with ITU-T Q.24 twist limits

interface.GoertzelDetector(sampleRate, params): Constructor with GoertzelParams (energyThreshold, normalTwistDb, reverseTwistDb, minDurationMs)
interface.detect(samples, sampleCount): Detect DTMF digit — returns GoertzelResult {detected, digit}
interface.detect(MediaBuffer&): Convenience — detects from a MediaBuffer's S16 PCM data

GoertzelDetector.handler: GoertzelHandler — IMediaHandler with separate detectors per direction, invokes DtmfCallback(char digit, uint32_t durationMs) on detection. Audio is forwarded unchanged (passive tap).
designNotes: Asymmetric twist limits per ITU-T Q.24: normalTwistDb=4.0 (high > low), reverseTwistDb=8.0 (low > high)
Duration tracking across frames with _currentDigit, _consecutiveMs, _lastReportedDigit
std::max(0.0, result) on magnitude prevents negative values from floating-point rounding
Separate detector instances per direction (incoming/outgoing) prevent cross-direction state

WavWriter.description: Header-only WAV file writer — writes standard RIFF WAV format with PCM data. Supports streaming writes with deferred header finalization.

interface.open(filePath): Open file for writing — writes initial WAV header with placeholder sizes. Returns bool.
interface.write(samples, sampleCount): Write S16 PCM samples to the data chunk. Returns bool.
interface.write(MediaBuffer&): Convenience — write a MediaBuffer's S16 PCM data. Rejects odd-byte buffers.
interface.close(): Finalize WAV header (seek back and write actual data/file sizes) and close file
interface.samplesWritten(): Total sample count written so far
interface.bytesWritten(): Total data bytes written so far
interface.durationMs(): Recording duration in milliseconds based on samples written and sample rate

WavWriter.handler: WavRecorderHandler — IMediaHandler passive tap that writes audio to a WAV file AND forwards buffers unchanged to the next handler. Does not modify the media stream.

handlerInterface.WavRecorderHandler(params, filePath, dir: Constructor — opens WAV file immediately. RecordDirection selects which direction(s) to record.
handlerInterface.startRecording(filePath): Start or restart recording to a new file
handlerInterface.stopRecording(): Stop recording and finalize WAV file

recordDirections.INCOMING: Record only incoming audio (received from remote)
recordDirections.OUTGOING: Record only outgoing audio (sent to remote)
recordDirections.BOTH: Record both directions — interleaves into a single mono file. Dual-file mode (separate files per direction) is a future enhancement.

designNotes: 44-byte RIFF WAV header: ChunkID='RIFF', Format='WAVE', Subchunk1ID='fmt ', Subchunk2ID='data'
AudioFormat=1 (PCM), little-endian byte order throughout
Header written on open() with placeholder sizes (0xFFFFFFFF), updated on close() by seeking back to offsets 4 and 40
If close() is not called (crash, etc.), the WAV file is still readable by most players that tolerate incorrect size fields
Passive tap pattern: WavRecorderHandler writes to disk but always forwards the buffer unchanged — it is invisible to downstream stages
File I/O uses std::ofstream — not on the real-time hot path for typical call recording use cases, but callers should be aware of potential disk I/O latency

WavReader.description: Header-only WAV file reader — parses RIFF WAV headers and reads PCM audio data into MediaBuffers

interface.open(filePath): Open and parse WAV file header. Returns bool.
interface.read(sampleCount): Read N samples into a new MediaBuffer. Returns nullptr at EOF.
interface.readAll(): Read entire file into a single MediaBuffer
interface.info(): Returns WavFileInfo {sampleRate, channels, bitsPerSample, totalSamples, durationMs, dataOffset}
interface.seek(sampleOffset): Seek to a sample position within the data chunk
interface.remaining(): Number of samples remaining to read

designNotes: Validates RIFF/WAVE/fmt /data chunk IDs and AudioFormat=1 (PCM only)
Supports 8-bit, 16-bit, and 24-bit PCM (bitsPerSample validation)
Skips unknown chunks between 'fmt ' and 'data' for robustness
WavFileInfo struct provides metadata without reading audio data
read(N) returns a MediaBuffer with up to N samples — may return fewer at end of file

PixelFormat.notes: P010 is 10-bit 4:2:0 planar format used by AV1 and H.265 HDR profiles. Included to support future HDR video pipelines without breaking the format enum.

SampleFormat.description: Audio sample format descriptors and conversion utilities. Little-endian only — all target platforms (x86, x86_64, ARM/AArch64) are little-endian. Big-endian variants (S16BE, S32BE, F32BE) are omitted to avoid dead code and untestable paths. If a big-endian platform is ever needed, add BE variants at that time with actual hardware to test on.
SampleFormat.notes: S16/S32/F32 are implicitly little-endian (no LE suffix needed since BE is not supported). MULAW and ALAW are single-byte encodings — endianness does not apply.

IMediaHandler.description: Base interface for media pipeline processing nodes — chainable handlers that transform MediaBuffers

interface.incoming(mediaBuffer): Process media arriving from an external source toward the application (e.g., received → decoded)
interface.outgoing(mediaBuffer): Process media going from the application toward an external sink (e.g., raw → encoded)
interface.addToChain(next): Chain this handler to the next handler

IMediaHandler.notes: Generic chain-of-responsibility pattern. incoming() and outgoing() execute synchronously in the caller's thread — no hidden queuing or thread hops on the hot path. This keeps latency predictable and avoids lock contention. If a handler needs async processing (e.g., video encode on a worker thread), it must explicitly dispatch to ThreadPool and return immediately. Transport bridge libraries (e.g., a future libdatachannel adapter) can wrap IMediaHandler to integrate with their own pipeline models.

TranscodingHandler.description: Media handler that decodes from one codec, optionally resamples, and re-encodes to another codec

AudioMixerHandler.description: IMediaHandler that mixes N audio input streams into per-participant N-1 output streams for conference calling

interface.AudioMixerHandler(params, bufferPool): Constructor with MixParams and optional shared buffer pool
interface.addParticipant(ssrc): Register a participant identified by RTP SSRC
interface.incoming(buffer): Receive decoded PCM from a participant (identified by buffer SSRC metadata), buffer it, and trigger mixing when all participants have contributed a frame

designNotes: incoming() identifies the source participant via MediaBuffer::ssrc() metadata
Per-participant bounded frame buffer (max 2-3 frames) absorbs arrival jitter
When all active participants have at least one buffered frame, triggers a mix round
For each participant, outputs an N-1 mix (all OTHER participants) via per-participant output chains
Late/missing frames: skip the participant for this mix round rather than blocking. PLC integration is a Phase 2 enhancement.
NOT thread-safe with respect to addParticipant/removeParticipant and mixing — caller must pause media flow during topology changes

Output routing: each participant has its own downstream IMediaHandler chain (e.g., encode → RTP packetize → send). AudioMixerHandler maintains an internal map of participantId → output handler. Mixed output is forwarded by calling outputHandler->incoming(mixedBuffer) directly — it does NOT use IMediaHandler::_next. AudioMixerHandler is a fan-in/fan-out terminal node, not a linear chain link.

Mixing cadence: timer-driven at a fixed interval (e.g., 20ms matching the codec ptime). On each tick, mix whatever frames are available per participant. Missing participants are skipped (silence). This avoids jitter accumulation from waiting for all participants to contribute.

MediaPipeline.description: Orchestrator that connects codec handlers, DSP stages, and external sources/sinks into a complete processing graph
features: Named pipeline stages with typed connections
Metrics collection per stage (latency, frame count, drop count)

Graph topology support: fan-out (one source to multiple sinks, e.g., recording + playback) and fan-in (multiple sources to one sink, e.g., conference mixing via AudioMixerHandler) in addition to linear chains. The pipeline is a directed acyclic graph (DAG), not just a chain. AudioMixerHandler is the initial fan-in implementation — it buffers N participant streams and outputs per-participant N-1 mixes. Full DAG topology with named ports and dynamic routing is a future enhancement.

integrationPoints.description: iora_codecs exposes clean interfaces at its boundaries so that separate transport/signaling libraries can plug in. The library does NOT depend on any transport library.

inboundInterface.description: Higher-level libraries (SIP stack, WebRTC bridge, etc.) feed raw media data into iora_codecs
entryPoints: IMediaHandler::incoming(MediaBuffer) — push received/depacketized media into the pipeline
CodecRegistry — transport libraries query available codecs for SDP negotiation

outboundInterface.description: iora_codecs delivers processed media back to transport libraries
exitPoints: IMediaHandler::outgoing(MediaBuffer) — delivers encoded/processed frames ready for packetization
Pipeline callbacks — std::function-based callbacks for frame-ready notifications

codecModules.description: Every codec module is a full Iora plugin — a shared library (.so/.dylib) that inherits from iora::IoraService::Plugin, implements onLoad()/onUnload(), and is declared via the IORA_DECLARE_PLUGIN macro. This is mandatory, not optional. Each codec plugin registers its ICodecFactory with the CodecRegistry during onLoad() and unregisters during onUnload(). Plugins are loaded by Iora's PluginManager from the directory specified in TOML configuration using RTLD_LOCAL (symbol isolation — shared state unified through libiora-core.so linkage). All codec dependencies use permissive licenses (BSD, Apache-2.0, Public domain) except bcg729 (GPL-3.0), which is confined to the optional mod_g729.so module. For embedded/single-binary deployments, IORA_CODECS_STATIC_PLUGINS=ON (not yet implemented) would compile codec modules as statically-linked Iora plugins.

pluginContract.description: Every codec module MUST follow this contract to be a valid Iora plugin

requiredOverrides.onLoad(iora::IoraService* service): Called by Iora when the plugin is loaded. The codec plugin MUST: (1) create its ICodecFactory instance, (2) register it with CodecRegistry via service->callExportedApi or direct registry access, (3) optionally export codec-specific APIs via service->exportApi() for runtime configuration
requiredOverrides.onUnload(): Called by Iora when the plugin is unloaded. The codec plugin MUST: (1) unregister its ICodecFactory from CodecRegistry, (2) release all codec instances and resources

pluginContract.requiredMacro: IORA_DECLARE_PLUGIN(ClassName) — must appear at the end of the plugin .cpp file to export the loadModule() symbol for dynamic loading

optionalOverrides.onDependencyLoaded(const std::string& mo: Notified when a dependency becomes available (e.g., a DSP plugin this codec depends on)
optionalOverrides.onDependencyUnloaded(const std::string&: Notified when a dependency is removed — codec should gracefully degrade

pluginContract.apiExport: Codec plugins export their ICodecFactory and runtime-configurable parameters via service->exportApi(*this, name, func) (Plugin& overload for automatic cleanup on unload). Encode/decode are NOT exported as APIs — they are accessed via C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec* instances created by the factory. This avoids API name collisions when multiple encoder instances of the same codec exist (e.g., two Opus encoders for two concurrent calls). The caller's choice of API retrieval method depends on call frequency — see apiAccessPatterns below.

example_plugin.description: Skeleton of a codec plugin (e.g., iora_codec_opus). The plugin registers its factory and exports config APIs. Encode/decode are NOT exported as APIs — they are C++ virtual calls on ICodec* instances created by the factory.
pseudocode: // Register factory with CodecRegistry via exported API
// Export the factory itself — callers retrieve via getExportedApi (~2ns)
// for fast codec instance creation at session setup
// Export config APIs — callers retrieve via getExportedApiSafe (~25ns, thread-safe)
// These operate on a SPECIFIC ICodec instance passed by the caller,
// not a plugin-global singleton (supports multiple concurrent sessions)
// exportApi(*this, ...) APIs are automatically unregistered by Iora
// because Plugin& overload tracks them in _apiExports for cleanup.
// NOTE: No _encoder/_decoder here — each media session creates its own
// Multiple concurrent sessions each hold their own ICodec*.

example_caller.description: How the pipeline creates codec instances and accesses APIs. Encode/decode use ICodec* virtual dispatch (~1-2ns). Config uses getExportedApiSafe (~25ns). Factory/registry queries use callExportedApi (~120ns).
pseudocode: // === Session setup (once per call, ~120ns — callExportedApi is fine) ===
// Create per-session codec instances via CodecRegistry
// Cache config APIs via getExportedApiSafe (~25ns per call, thread-safe)
// C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec* — ~1-2ns, NO exported API overhead,
// no locks, no lookups, no std::function indirection.
// This is the hot path — encode/decode are virtual method calls, not API calls.
// === Occasional config changes (called ~1/sec or on events) ===
// Passes the specific ICodec* instance to the exported API lambda
(*setFecFn)(encoder, packetLossHigh);  // enable FEC when loss > threshold
delete encoder;  // or return to pool

configuration.description: Codec plugins are loaded via Iora's TOML configuration, just like any other Iora plugin

apiAccessPatterns.description: Iora provides three API access methods with very different performance characteristics. Each iora_codecs API call site MUST use the correct method based on call frequency. Using callExportedApi on the hot path is a performance bug — 60x slower than getExportedApi.

getExportedApi<Sig>(name).mechanism: Returns std::function<Sig> via one-time lock + hash lookup + std::any_cast. After retrieval, calling the returned std::function is a direct function pointer dereference — essentially zero overhead. The std::function is a local copy that the caller caches and reuses.
getExportedApi<Sig>(name).threadSafety: The retrieval itself is mutex-protected, but the returned std::function has NO module-state validation. If the plugin is unloaded while the caller holds the reference, calling it is undefined behavior (dangling pointer). Safe when codec plugins are loaded once at startup and never unloaded during the session lifetime — which is the normal case for media pipelines.
getExportedApi<Sig>(name).useFor: CACHED RETRIEVAL — factory accessors and any plugin API that is called repeatedly and whose plugin will not be unloaded. Cache the std::function at session setup and reuse it. NOTE: encode()/decode()/plc() are NOT exported APIs — they are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec* (even faster, ~1-2ns vtable lookup). getExportedApi is for retrieving plugin-exported functions like factories.

getExportedApiSafe<Sig>(name).mechanism: Returns shared_ptr<SafeApiFunction<Sig>>. On each call, SafeApiFunction::operator() performs: (1) atomic valid flag check (~1ns), (2) isModuleLoaded() which acquires _loadModulesMutex + hash lookup (~10-15ns), (3) cacheMutex lock with double-checked locking (~10-15ns), (4) cached function call (~2ns). Note: two mutexes are acquired on the fast path (_loadModulesMutex in isModuleLoaded + cacheMutex). The ~25ns figure is measured under zero contention — under heavy multi-thread contention, cost can spike to microseconds due to mutex wait. If the module was reloaded, it transparently refreshes the cached std::function via getExportedApi internally. Uses weak_ptr event handler to auto-invalidate cache on module unload.
getExportedApiSafe<Sig>(name).threadSafety: Fully thread-safe. Handles concurrent calls from multiple threads and survives module reload/unload. The shared_ptr ensures the SafeApiFunction outlives any pending calls.
getExportedApiSafe<Sig>(name).useFor: MEDIUM-FREQUENCY — runtime parameter changes (setBitrate, setComplexity, enableFec, requestKeyFrame) that happen occasionally during a call but need multi-thread safety. Also appropriate for cross-plugin calls where the callee might be reloaded.

callExportedApi<Ret, Args...>(name, args.mechanism: Full lookup every call: debug log (~10-30ns) + mutex lock (~20ns) + hash lookup (~5ns) + std::any_cast (~2ns) + function call (~2ns). No caching whatsoever — each invocation pays the full cost of name resolution, type validation, and lock acquisition.
callExportedApi<Ret, Args...>(name, args.threadSafety: Thread-safe by virtue of per-call locking. Each call is independent.
callExportedApi<Ret, Args...>(name, args.useFor: INFREQUENT — one-time setup/teardown operations: createEncoder(), createDecoder(), registerFactory(), unregisterFactory(), enumerateCodecs(), findByName(). These happen once per session or once per call setup, so 120ns is negligible.

callSiteMapping.description: Concrete mapping of iora_codecs operations to the correct access method. IMPORTANT: encode/decode/plc are NOT exported APIs — they are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec* instances. Only factory retrieval and config parameters are exported APIs.

hotPath_virtualDispatch.frequency: 50-8000+ calls/sec per stream (depends on codec frame size)
hotPath_virtualDispatch.overhead: ~1-2ns per call (C++ vtable lookup, no API system involvement)
operations: encoder->encode(buffer) — C++ virtual call on ICodec*, called every codec frame (e.g., every 20ms = 50/sec for Opus)
decoder->decode(buffer) — C++ virtual call on ICodec*, same frequency as encode
decoder->plc(frameSamples) — C++ virtual call on ICodec*, called on packet loss
resampler.process(input, output) — direct C++ call, per frame when bridging sample rates
hotPath_virtualDispatch._note_rtp_hotpath: RTP hot-path operations (jitterBuffer.put/get, RtpPacket::parse/build) are part of the iora_rtp library, not iora_codecs. They share the same zero-overhead design principles (direct C++ calls, no API system involvement).
hotPath_virtualDispatch.pattern: At session setup, create per-session ICodec* instances via CodecRegistry::createEncoder()/createDecoder(). Store the ICodec* in the pipeline session state. Call encode()/decode()/plc() as virtual method calls for the lifetime of the session. These calls never touch the Iora exported API system — zero overhead beyond a vtable lookup.
hotPath_virtualDispatch.whyNotExportedApi: Encode/decode cannot be exported APIs because: (1) each session needs its own stateful codec instance — exporting a global 'opus.encode' would be shared across all sessions, which is not thread-safe for stateful codecs; (2) virtual dispatch (~1-2ns) is faster than even getExportedApi's std::function call (~2ns + function pointer dereference); (3) the ICodec interface provides type-safe C++ semantics without std::any_cast overhead.

mediumPath_getExportedApi.frequency: Once per session setup, then cached
operations: opus.factory() — retrieve the ICodecFactory* for fast codec instance creation
Other factory accessors for each codec type
mediumPath_getExportedApi.pattern: Retrieve the std::function once via getExportedApi at session setup and cache it. The factory itself is stateless, so a single cached reference is safe across all sessions. Codec plugins are loaded at startup and never unloaded mid-session, so the dangling-reference risk of getExportedApi does not apply.
mediumPath_getExportedApi.safety: Codec plugins are loaded once at startup and remain loaded for the process lifetime. Mid-session codec hot-swap creates NEW ICodec instances from the existing factory rather than reloading the plugin .so.

operations: opus.setBitrate(bps) — adaptive bitrate changes based on network conditions
opus.setComplexity(level) — may change based on CPU load
opus.enableFec(bool) — toggled based on packet loss rate
h264.requestKeyFrame() / vpx.requestKeyFrame() / av1.requestKeyFrame() — triggered by transport layer on PLI/FIR
mediumFrequency_getExportedApiSafe.pattern: Retrieve SafeApiFunction once at session setup via getExportedApiSafe, cache the shared_ptr, call operator() when parameter changes are needed. The ~25ns overhead is irrelevant at this frequency but the thread safety is important since these may be called from network threads, timer callbacks, or control plane threads concurrently.

infrequent_callExportedApi.frequency: Once per session or once per call setup
operations: codecs.registerFactory(factory) — during plugin onLoad(), once per codec type
codecs.createEncoder(info) — once per media session setup
codecs.createDecoder(info) — once per media session setup
codecs.enumerateCodecs() — during SDP offer/answer in signaling library
infrequent_callExportedApi.pattern: Call directly via service.callExportedApi<Ret, Args...>(name, args...). No caching needed — 120ns is irrelevant for operations that happen once.

iora_codec_opus.hotPath: encode()/decode()/plc() are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec* — NOT exported APIs
iora_codec_opus.notes: Mandatory for modern VoIP and WebRTC applications.

iora_codec_g711.hotPath: encode()/decode() are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec* — NOT exported APIs
iora_codec_g711.notes: Inline implementation — no external library. Still an Iora plugin for consistency. Always available. No configurable parameters (fixed 64 kbps).

iora_codec_g722.hotPath: encode()/decode() are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec*

iora_codec_ilbc.hotPath: encode()/decode()/plc() are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec*

iora_codec_amr.hotPath: encode()/decode() are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec*
iora_codec_amr.notes: Patent license from Via Licensing may be required.

iora_codec_g729.license: GPL-3.0 (isolated — mod_g729.so only, loaded with RTLD_LOCAL)
iora_codec_g729.hotPath: encode()/decode()/plc() are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec*
iora_codec_g729.notes: GPL-3.0 scope confined to mod_g729.so via RTLD_LOCAL + static bcg729 linkage. OFF by default (IORA_CODECS_ENABLE_G729=OFF). G.729 Annex A is fully interoperable with G.729; SDP name is 'G729' per RFC 3551. No runtime-configurable parameters (fixed 8 kbps). Patents expired 2017.

iora_codec_h264.hotPath: encode()/decode() are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec*
iora_codec_h264.notes: Prefer Cisco prebuilt binaries for royalty-free usage.

iora_codec_vpx.hotPath: encode()/decode() are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec*
iora_codec_vpx.notes: Single plugin registers two factories (VP8 + VP9), following the AMR multi-variant pattern. Config APIs apply to both variants.

iora_codec_av1.hotPath: encode()/decode() are C++ virtual dispatch on ICodec*
iora_codec_av1.notes: CPU-intensive. Optional for forward-looking deployments. Encoder uses libaom with AOM_USAGE_REALTIME. Decoder uses dav1d for superior performance.

iora_codecs/..gitmodules: Git submodule definitions for all external dependencies
iora_codecs/.CMakeLists.txt: Top-level build — add_subdirectory/ExternalProject for submodules, build options per codec

cmake/.SubmoduleTargets.cmake: CMake wrappers for non-CMake submodules (autotools, Meson) via ExternalProject_Add

libs/.speexdsp/: Git submodule — libspeexdsp (only resample.c compiled directly, no autotools/ExternalProject)

src/.dsp/: Resampler (wraps libspeexdsp), AudioMixer — header-only DSP components (Gain, VAD, ToneGenerator, GoertzelDetector, WavWriter, WavReader) have no .cpp files

modules/.codec_registry/: mod_codec_registry plugin — owns and exports the central CodecRegistry; must be loaded before codec modules

tests/.core/: Core abstraction tests (SampleFormat, PixelFormat, CodecInfo, MediaBuffer, MediaClock, CodecRegistry)
tests/.codec/: Per-codec encode/decode round-trip tests and plugin integration tests
tests/.dsp/: DSP utility tests (Resampler, Gain, VAD, ToneGenerator, GoertzelDetector, WavWriter, WavReader, integration)

IORA_CODECS_ENABLE_AMR.description: Build AMR codec module (patent encumbered — Via Licensing patent license is effectively mandatory for commercial deployment, not optional. Default OFF to avoid legal risk for developers unaware of the licensing requirement)

IORA_CODECS_STATIC_PLUGINS.description: (NOT YET IMPLEMENTED — design placeholder) Link codec modules statically instead of as runtime-loadable plugins. Useful for embedded/single-binary deployments.

minimumDependencies.description: A minimal build requires only: Iora + libopus + libspeexdsp + G.711 (built-in). All other codecs (G.722, iLBC, H.264, VP8/VP9) are ON by default; AMR and AV1 are OFF by default. Each can be individually enabled/disabled. The 'mandatory' flag in codecModules indicates codecs recommended for production VoIP — not that they cannot be disabled.

usageScenariosAndDataFlow._note_cross_library: These scenarios span multiple libraries. Steps marked [iora_rtp] are handled by the companion iora_rtp library, [iora_io_device] by the device I/O library, and [iora_codecs] by this library. The boundaries: iora_rtp delivers encoded MediaBuffers to iora_codecs for decoding and receives encoded MediaBuffers back; iora_io_device delivers raw MediaBuffers (captured audio/video) and receives decoded MediaBuffers for playback.

scenario1_audio_transcode.description: Transcode between two audio codecs (e.g., Opus ↔ G.711) — the core SBC use case
dataFlow: 1. [iora_rtp] Transport library receives RTP packets → RtpPacket::parse() → JitterBuffer reorders and triggers playout
2. [iora_rtp] JitterBuffer delivers MediaBuffer (encoded payload) to TranscodingHandler
3. [iora_codecs] TranscodingHandler: OpusDecoder.decode() → PCM(48kHz)
4. [iora_codecs] TranscodingHandler: Resampler.process() → PCM(8kHz)
5. [iora_codecs] TranscodingHandler: G711Encoder.encode() → G.711 payload
6. [iora_rtp] RtpPacket::build() assembles outbound RTP bytes → returned to transport library for sending
7. Reverse path: G.711 → decode → resample(8kHz→48kHz) → Opus encode

scenario2_audio_capture_encode.description: Capture audio from a device, encode, and deliver encoded frames to a transport library
dataFlow: 1. [iora_io_device] IAudioDevice (ALSA) captures PCM(48kHz, S16LE) from microphone
2. [iora_io_device] Device callback delivers MediaBuffer to pipeline
3. [iora_codecs] Pipeline routes through: VAD → Gain → OpusEncoder
4. [iora_codecs] Encoded MediaBuffer delivered via pipeline outgoing callback
5. Transport library (external) receives encoded frames for RTP packetization and sending

scenario3_audio_decode_playback.description: Receive encoded audio from a transport library, decode, and play back on a device
dataFlow: 1. [iora_rtp] Transport library feeds encoded MediaBuffer → JitterBuffer reorders and schedules playout
2. [iora_codecs] OpusDecoder.decode() → PCM(48kHz, S16LE)
3. [iora_codecs] Gain adjustment if needed
4. [iora_io_device] IAudioDevice (ALSA) plays PCM on speaker

scenario4_dtmf_relay.description: DTMF digit relay through the media pipeline — both RFC 4733 telephone-event and in-band tone detection/generation
dataFlow: 1. [iora_rtp] Inbound: Transport library passes RTP with PT=101 (telephone-event) to DtmfHandler
2. [iora_rtp] DtmfHandler decodes RFC 4733 payload → emits typed MediaEvent{DtmfDigit{digit, duration, volume}}
3. [iora_rtp] Pipeline routes event: if outbound codec supports telephone-event, re-encode as RFC 4733 on outbound RTP
4. [iora_codecs] If outbound codec does NOT support telephone-event, ToneGenerator generates in-band DTMF tones injected into the PCM pipeline
5. [iora_codecs] In-band detection (reverse): Goertzel detector monitors decoded PCM for DTMF tones → emits MediaEvent if detected

scenario5_codec_change_midcall.description: Mid-call codec renegotiation (e.g., Opus → G.711 after SDP re-INVITE) without audible glitch
dataFlow: 1. Signaling library (external) completes SDP renegotiation, determines new codec
2. Signaling library calls MediaPipeline::swapCodec(direction, newCodecInfo)
3. Pipeline drains in-flight frames from old codec (ILifecycleManaged::Draining state)
4. Pipeline creates new encoder/decoder via CodecRegistry::createEncoder/createDecoder
5. If sample rates differ, Resampler is inserted/removed/reconfigured automatically
6. New codec enters Running state — seamless transition, no audible gap beyond one frame period

scenario6_video_encode_decode.description: Capture video from a device, encode with H.264, and decode received H.264 for display
dataFlow: 1. [iora_io_device] IVideoDevice (V4L2) captures I420 frames from camera
2. [iora_codecs] H264Encoder.encode() → H.264 NAL units in MediaBuffer
3. [iora_codecs] Encoded MediaBuffer delivered via pipeline outgoing callback to transport library
4. Receive path: transport library feeds H.264 NAL units into pipeline
5. [iora_codecs] H264Decoder.decode() → I420 frames
6. [iora_io_device] IVideoDevice display renders decoded frames

scenario7_audio_conference.description: N-way audio conference where each participant hears a mix of all OTHER participants (N-1 mix semantics)
dataFlow: 1. [iora_rtp] Each participant's RTP stream → JitterBuffer reorders and triggers playout
2. [iora_codecs] Per-participant TranscodingHandler decodes and resamples to common rate (e.g., 16 kHz mono PCM)
3. [iora_codecs] AudioMixerHandler.incoming(buffer) — identifies participant by SSRC, buffers the frame
4. [iora_codecs] When all active participants have contributed a frame, AudioMixer produces N-1 mixes (participant A hears B+C, B hears A+C, C hears A+B) using efficient subtract-from-total algorithm
5. [iora_codecs] Each participant's N-1 mix output is forwarded to their downstream TranscodingHandler for re-encoding to the participant's outbound codec
6. [iora_rtp] RtpPacketBuilder packetizes encoded output for each participant
designNotes: Upstream decode pipelines (step 2) are per-participant linear IMediaHandler chains feeding into the shared AudioMixerHandler
Downstream encode pipelines (step 5) are per-participant linear chains routed by AudioMixerHandler's participant→outputHandler map
All participants normalized to same sample rate BEFORE mixing — Resampler instances live in upstream TranscodingHandlers, not in the mixer itself
MediaBufferPool shared across all participants — sized for (maxParticipants × 2 frames) + headroom

scenario8_call_recording.description: Record audio from a live call to a WAV file — passive tap that does not modify the media stream
dataFlow: 1. [iora_rtp] RTP stream → JitterBuffer → playout
2. [iora_codecs] TranscodingHandler decodes to S16 PCM
3. [iora_codecs] WavRecorderHandler taps the pipeline — writes S16 PCM to .wav file AND forwards buffer unchanged
4. [iora_codecs] Downstream stages (Gain, VAD, etc.) receive unmodified audio — recording is transparent
5. On call end or recording stop: WavRecorderHandler.stopRecording() finalizes WAV header with correct data sizes
designNotes: WavRecorderHandler is a passive tap — it inserts anywhere in the pipeline without affecting audio processing
RecordDirection enum controls which direction(s) to capture: INCOMING (remote audio), OUTGOING (local audio), or BOTH
BOTH mode interleaves into a single mono file (dual-file mode is a future enhancement)
Recording can be started/stopped mid-call via startRecording()/stopRecording()
If the process crashes without calling close(), the WAV file is still playable by most applications that tolerate incorrect size headers

unitTests: Codec round-trip: encode(decode(original)) == original (within lossy codec tolerance)
Resampler: rate conversion accuracy (48kHz→8kHz→48kHz), quality levels, stereo, edge cases
MediaBuffer: pool allocation, reference counting, metadata preservation
Core abstractions: SampleFormat, PixelFormat, CodecInfo, MediaClock
Gain: unity/double/half/zero gain, saturation clamping, dB conversion, mute/unmute, NaN/infinity rejection
VAD: silence/speech detection, threshold sensitivity, hangover logic, RMS/peak calculation, reset, DROP_SILENT/MARK_ONLY modes
ToneGenerator: buffer sizing, amplitude scaling, all 16 DTMF digits, frequency verification via Goertzel, sample rates, sequences
GoertzelDetector: all 16 digits, silence/noise rejection, twist limits, duration tracking, minimum duration enforcement, sample rates
WavWriter: header correctness, streaming writes, close/finalize, duration tracking, odd-byte rejection, large file support
WavReader: header parsing, full/partial reads, seek, format validation, chunk skipping, EOF handling
testingStrategy._note_rtp_tests: RTP/RTCP, JitterBuffer, DtmfHandler, and RingBuffer tests belong in the iora_rtp library test suite.
integrationTests: TranscodingHandler: Opus↔G.711 end-to-end with reference audio file comparison
AudioMixerHandler: N-way conference mixing with N-1 output verification per participant
Full pipeline: encode→decode loopback with reference audio comparison
DSP pipeline: VAD→Gain, ToneGenerator→GoertzelDetector round-trip, multi-stage VAD+Gain+Goertzel
WavWriter→WavReader round-trip: write PCM to WAV, read back, verify sample-exact match
WavRecorderHandler in pipeline: verify recording captures audio without modifying downstream output
performanceBenchmarks: Codec latency: measure encode+decode time per frame for each codec at target sample rate (must be < 50% of frame duration for real-time)
Pipeline throughput: frames/second through full transcode pipeline (Opus→G.711 and H.264→VP8)
Memory allocation: verify zero heap allocations in steady-state hot path (after pool warmup)
Resampler: CPU cost per second of audio at various quality levels (0-10)

iora_rtp.description: RTP framing, jitter buffering, DTMF, and comfort noise — companion library to iora_codecs. Handles RTP packet parsing/building (RFC 3550), RTCP (RFC 3550/4585), header extensions (RFC 5285), JitterBuffer, DtmfHandler (RFC 4733), and ComfortNoiseHandler (RFC 3389). Depends on iora_codecs_core for shared types (MediaBuffer, CodecInfo, MediaClock). No codec modules depend on iora_rtp.

components.RtpPacket: RFC 3550 RTP packet parsing/building with RFC 5285 header extensions
components.RtcpPacket: RFC 3550/4585 RTCP compound packet parsing/building (SR, RR, SDES, BYE, NACK, PLI)
components.JitterBuffer: Adaptive jitter buffer for RTP packet reordering and playout scheduling
components.DtmfHandler: RFC 4733 telephone-event RTP payload generation and detection
components.ComfortNoiseHandler: RFC 3389 comfort noise generation and processing

iora_rtp.rationale: RTP is a transport framing protocol, not a codec concern. Separating it: (1) keeps iora_codecs focused on encode/decode/DSP, (2) allows iora_rtp to be used independently (e.g., RTP proxy/relay without codecs), (3) provides a natural home for future transport features (SRTP, DTLS, RED, FEC), (4) maintains a clean dependency graph where codec modules never depend on RTP.

iora_io_device.description: Audio/video device I/O — capture and playback hardware abstraction. Platform-specific drivers (ALSA, PulseAudio, PipeWire, V4L2, CoreAudio, WASAPI) loaded as plugins. Depends on iora_codecs_core for shared types (MediaBuffer, SampleFormat, PixelFormat).

components.IAudioDevice: Abstract interface for audio capture and playback devices with ILifecycleManaged lifecycle
components.IVideoDevice: Abstract interface for video capture and display devices
components.DeviceManager: Device enumeration, hot-plug detection, default device selection

iora_io_device.rationale: Device I/O is platform-specific hardware abstraction, not a codec concern. Separating it: (1) keeps iora_codecs focused on encode/decode/DSP, (2) allows iora_io_device to be used independently for audio/video capture without codecs, (3) isolates platform-specific code (ALSA, PulseAudio, V4L2) from the portable codec library, (4) future platform drivers (PipeWire, CoreAudio, WASAPI) extend iora_io_device without touching iora_codecs.

iora_webrtc.description: WebRTC integration — libdatachannel bridge with adapter wrapping IMediaHandler as rtc::MediaHandler

futureConsiderations: iora_rtp transport extensions: SRTP/DTLS-SRTP session management, built on Iora's UnifiedSharedTransport
RED (RFC 2198) — Redundant Audio Data for packet loss recovery in iora_rtp. Near-term priority: RED is widely used in WebRTC for Opus.
FlexFEC (RFC 8627) — Flexible Forward Error Correction for video streams in iora_rtp.
RTP header extensions (RFC 5285) — specific extension type parsers in iora_rtp: abs-send-time, audio level indication (RFC 6464), mid/rid for simulcast.
Hardware codec acceleration (VAAPI, NVENC, V4L2 M2M for video encode/decode)
Acoustic echo cancellation (AEC) using libspeexdsp or WebRTC AEC3
Noise suppression (NS) using RNNoise or libspeexdsp
Conference mixing enhancements: dominant speaker detection, VAD-based silence exclusion, AGC normalization — builds on AudioMixer/AudioMixerHandler
Simulcast and SVC layer selection for video
Bandwidth estimation (TWCC-based) for adaptive bitrate — requires abs-send-time RTP header extension in iora_rtp
RTCP Extended Reports (RFC 3611) for VoIP quality metrics (MOS estimation) in iora_rtp
EVS codec support if open-source implementation becomes available
PipeWire audio device driver for modern Linux desktops (in iora_io_device)
macOS CoreAudio and Windows WASAPI device drivers (in iora_io_device)
